aiTitle: Remove reserve_slot from consumer and producer

content.content:
259	would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
260	`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
261	the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
262	protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
263	(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).
264	
265	Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
266	the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
267	single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.
268	
269	## Blocking and user words
270	
271	The crate never blocks and never spins. `reserve_slot`
272	returning [`Full`]/[`Empty`] immediately is the entire API;
273	what to do about it — spin, yield, park, await — is policy,
274	and policy belongs to the layer above (an embedded caller may
275	WFE on an interrupt, an async runtime wants a waker, a pinned
276	busy-poll thread wants a pure spin; no single crate-blessed
277	hybrid fits all three).
278	
279	What the crate provides is mechanism: the header's `user`
280	line — 16 `AtomicU32` words, zeroed by `Ring::init`, exposed
281	via `user()` on both endpoints, and never read, written, or
282	interpreted by the crate afterwards. It is the well-known
283	shared-memory home where independently written peers can
284	build a wakeup protocol (waiter flags, futex words, sequence
285	numbers). `AtomicU32` deliberately: futex-shaped, portable to
286	targets without 64-bit atomics, race-free under concurrent
287	peer access.
288	
289	Contracts and cautions for that layer:
290	
291	- **Lost-wakeup discipline** — the sleep side must re-check
292	  the ring *after* publishing its "wake me" flag and before
293	  sleeping (`set flag → reserve_slot once more → sleep`), and
294	  the wake side checks the flag *after* its commit/release
295	  (`commit → check flag → wake`). Skipping the re-check races
296	  a commit landing between flag-set and sleep: the waker saw
297	  no flag, the sleeper saw no message — it sleeps forever.
298	  futex-style primitives exist precisely to make the final
299	  check-and-sleep atomic.
300	- **Values, not addresses** — a pointer stored in the region
301	  is meaningless in the peer's address space (and a
302	  dereference of peer-writable bytes besides). Store data;
303	  keep pointers on your side of the wall.
304	- **Timeouts** — a peer can die while you sleep; every wait
305	  should carry a timeout so a dead producer degrades the
306	  consumer to periodic polling, not a hang.
307	
308	## Validation
309	
310	Per-commit, alongside the cargo cycle:
311	
312	- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite, threaded test
313	  included (its message count is reduced under `cfg(miri)`;
314	  interpreted spin loops are slow). Miri has caught two real
315	  Stacked Borrows violations: the init retag (0.3.0-4) and
316	  the guard argument-protector race (0.3.0-6). The latter
317	  reproduces only when the threaded test runs, so the full
318	  suite — not a non-threaded subset — stays in the ladder.
319	- `indices_survive_u32_wrap` pins the free-running-index wrap
320	  argument (fill/drain across `u32::MAX` with masked
321	  positions intact).
322	- [loom](https://docs.rs/loom) for exhaustive ordering
323	  exploration is a possible follow-on (Ideas).
324	
325	## Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues
326	
327	Design for the layer above the ring. The pool half is
328	as-built as of the 0.6.0 cycle (`src/pool.rs`: layout,
329	init/attach, alloc/free); descriptor queues and provenance
330	remain design. The sections above are the as-built record of
331	the ring itself.
332

file.content:
would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Blocking and user words

The crate never blocks and never spins. `reserve_slot`
returning [`Full`]/[`Empty`] immediately is the entire API;
what to do about it — spin, yield, park, await — is policy,
and policy belongs to the layer above (an embedded caller may
WFE on an interrupt, an async runtime wants a waker, a pinned
busy-poll thread wants a pure spin; no single crate-blessed
hybrid fits all three).

What the crate provides is mechanism: the header's `user`
line — 16 `AtomicU32` words, zeroed by `Ring::init`, exposed
via `user()` on both endpoints, and never read, written, or
interpreted by the crate afterwards. It is the well-known
shared-memory home where independently written peers can
build a wakeup protocol (waiter flags, futex words, sequence
numbers). `AtomicU32` deliberately: futex-shaped, portable to
targets without 64-bit atomics, race-free under concurrent
peer access.

Contracts and cautions for that layer:

- **Lost-wakeup discipline** — the sleep side must re-check
  the ring *after* publishing its "wake me" flag and before
  sleeping (`set flag → reserve_slot once more → sleep`), and
  the wake side checks the flag *after* its commit/release
  (`commit → check flag → wake`). Skipping the re-check races
  a commit landing between flag-set and sleep: the waker saw
  no flag, the sleeper saw no message — it sleeps forever.
  futex-style primitives exist precisely to make the final
  check-and-sleep atomic.
- **Values, not addresses** — a pointer stored in the region
  is meaningless in the peer's address space (and a
  dereference of peer-writable bytes besides). Store data;
  keep pointers on your side of the wall.
- **Timeouts** — a peer can die while you sleep; every wait
  should carry a timeout so a dead producer degrades the
  consumer to periodic polling, not a hang.

## Validation

Per-commit, alongside the cargo cycle:

- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite, threaded test
  included (its message count is reduced under `cfg(miri)`;
  interpreted spin loops are slow). Miri has caught two real
  Stacked Borrows violations: the init retag (0.3.0-4) and
  the guard argument-protector race (0.3.0-6). The latter
  reproduces only when the threaded test runs, so the full
  suite — not a non-threaded subset — stays in the ladder.
- `indices_survive_u32_wrap` pins the free-running-index wrap
  argument (fill/drain across `u32::MAX` with masked
  positions intact).
- [loom](https://docs.rs/loom) for exhaustive ordering
  exploration is a possible follow-on (Ideas).

## Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues

Design for the layer above the ring. The pool half is
as-built as of the 0.6.0 cycle (`src/pool.rs`: layout,
init/attach, alloc/free); descriptor queues and provenance
remain design. The sections above are the as-built record of
the ring itself.

The ring's `reserve_slot` fuses three acts that a messaging
system needs separated: it allocates message memory (the slot),
assigns queue position (the reservation is the ring head), and
opens a publication window (the guard exclusively borrows the
endpoint, blocking all other sends). Getting a message must not
imply sending it now — or ever. The fix is a layer, not a ring
rework: pools own message memory, queues carry small
descriptors, and the existing ring is the unchanged primitive
underneath (reserve-to-commit becomes a momentary act, where
the fusion is harmless).

### System model (overview)

The layer's rules at a glance; each bullet links to the
section that expands it.

content.text: Now the Producer/Consumer API bullets — rewrite to the two-rung ladder:

input.old_string:
Producer — reserve_slot/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>() ->
  Result<WriteSlot<'_, T>, Full>` — next free slot as `&mut T`
  (zerocopy `FromBytes + IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T`
  geometry is asserted against the slot size on each call (a
  mismatch is a programming error, so it panics; typed
  endpoints that check once are a follow-on — see
  [todo.md](todo.md)).
- `WriteSlot::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — reserve_slot/release, in place:

- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>() ->
  Result<ReadSlot<'_, T>, Empty>` — oldest unread slot as
  `&T`.
- `ReadSlot::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next reserve_slot returns it
  again.

Each side reserves at most **one slot at a time**: the guard
holds the endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
while a guard is live is a compile error, not a runtime state.
input.new_string:
Producer — reserve_slot_with/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_full) ->
  Result<WriteSlot<'_, T>, Full>` — next free slot as `&mut T`
  (zerocopy `FromBytes + IntoBytes + KnownLayout`), retrying
  under the injected wait policy — `on_full(attempt) -> bool`,
  `|_| false` for a single non-blocking probe. `T` geometry is
  asserted against the slot size on each call (a mismatch is a
  programming error, so it panics; typed endpoints that check
  once are a follow-on — see [todo.md](todo.md)).
- `producer.reserve_slot_spin::<T>() -> WriteSlot<'_, T>` — the
  forever-spin convenience (`reserve_slot_with` under
  `policy::spin`), so no `Result`.
- `WriteSlot::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — reserve_slot_with/release, in place:

- `consumer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_empty) ->
  Result<ReadSlot<'_, T>, Empty>` — oldest unread slot as
  `&T`, retrying under the injected wait policy (`|_| false`
  for a single non-blocking probe).
- `consumer.reserve_slot_spin::<T>() -> ReadSlot<'_, T>` — the
  forever-spin convenience, so no `Result`.
- `ReadSlot::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next reservation returns it
  again.

Each side reserves at most **one slot at a time**: the guard
holds the endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second reservation
while a guard is live is a compile error, not a runtime state.

content.text:
Demo runs clean at 0.8.0, exit 0 — the migrated `_with(|_| false)` (1t) and `reserve_slot_spin` (2t) paths all produce sane throughput. And a repo-wide grep confirms no bare `reserve_slot` method reference survives anywhere in `src/`, `examples/`, `README.md`, or the notes.

## Work complete — please review

Full diff is in `jj diff`. What changed:

**Code (the removal + migration):**
- `Cargo.toml` — `0.7.3` → `0.8.0`
- `src/consumer.rs`, `src/producer.rs` — deleted `reserve_slot`; `reserve_slot_with` is now the self-contained primitive (absorbed the raw-pointer/`>=`-corruption rationale + a `|_| false` single-probe note); repointed the `Empty`/`Full`, struct, `reserve_slot_spin`, and guard-`msg` doc links
- `src/lib.rs` — `check_type` comment; test single-probes → `_with(|_| false)`; `threaded_spsc` now loops on `_with(|_| false)` (keeps a fallible-primitive-under-contention test, distinct from `threaded_spsc_spin_variants`)
- `src/registry.rs` — test spin-loops → `reserve_slot_spin`; dropped now-unused `Empty`/`Full` imports
- `src/pool.rs` — one doc cross-reference
- `src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs` — `*_1t` → `_with(|_| false)`, `*_2t` → `reserve_slot_spin`; module doc

**Docs:**
- `examples/readme.rs`, `README.md` — snippets + prose to `_with(|_| false)` / the new API line
- `notes/todo.md` — two incidental mentions
- `notes/ring-buffer-design.md` — `## API` rewritten to the two-rung ladder

**One thing I want you to scrutinize:** in `ring-buffer-design.md`, the `## Blocking and user words` intro said *"The crate never blocks and never spins"* — that claim predates the wait-policy hook (the crate now spins via `reserve_slot_spin`), so a bare rename would have left an inline contradiction. I reconciled it to "the ring's fallible core never blocks… *how* to wait is injected policy… a shipped `policy::spin` drives `reserve_slot_spin`, but anything that actually sleeps belongs above." That's the one spot where I edited design rationale, not just a method name — check I framed it the way you'd want.

**Not yet done** (held until we settle the commit title, since they reuse it): the notes bookkeeping — remove bug #1 from `bugs.md`, add the `## Done` entry, and open/fill the chores section. Since you asked for a single commit, I'll fold that bookkeeping into the same `0.8.0` commit.

How's the diff look?

=== chores-01.md tail ===
  "fixed" later: pinning works on any core (diff-cores runs
  everywhere), but two threads simultaneously on one
  physical core *is* SMT hardware. A probe pinning both
  spin-waiting threads to one cpu measured ~8k msgs/sec vs
  ~12M cross-core — we think each handoff waits out a
  scheduler timeslice, so that number measures the
  scheduler, not the ring.

content.content:
524	## feat: demo seam lines on diff cores, SMT last
525	
526	Commits:
527	
528	On a CPU without SMT (Pi 5's Cortex-A76, one thread per
529	core) the same-core section skips, and the `_closure` /
530	`_spin` seam lines ran only there — so a non-SMT machine
531	never printed the three-way comparison under real waiting.
532	
533	- The diff-cores section now prints the full five-line set;
534	  on the Pi 5 the three forms land within a few percent of
535	  each other (~82–87 ns/msg across two A76 cores) — the

file.content:
## feat: demo seam lines on diff cores, SMT last

Commits:

On a CPU without SMT (Pi 5's Cortex-A76, one thread per
core) the same-core section skips, and the `_closure` /
`_spin` seam lines ran only there — so a non-SMT machine
never printed the three-way comparison under real waiting.

- The diff-cores section now prints the full five-line set;
  on the Pi 5 the three forms land within a few percent of
  each other (~82–87 ns/msg across two A76 cores) — the
  eyeball zero-cost the seam check wants.
- The same-core (SMT) section moved last: it's the only
  hardware-conditional section, so a machine without SMT
  ends with the notice instead of a hole mid-output.
- Skip notices print on two lines (header, then indented
  reason) instead of padding to the scoreboard columns.
- The skip itself is correct behavior, recorded so it isn't
  "fixed" later: pinning works on any core (diff-cores runs
  everywhere), but two threads simultaneously on one
  physical core *is* SMT hardware. A probe pinning both
  spin-waiting threads to one cpu measured ~8k msgs/sec vs
  ~12M cross-core — we think each handoff waits out a
  scheduler timeslice, so that number measures the
  scheduler, not the ring.

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/32fec004bd30 "32fec004bd300cc072a052fd0f80882a582c790f"

aiTitle: Remove reserve_slot from consumer and producer

content.text:
I have the conventions. Provisional commit title (chores header + Done entry reuse it): **`refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder`** (46 chars). Doing the bookkeeping now.

**1. Remove bug #1 from `bugs.md`:**

input.old_string:
1. `reserve_slot` (both `Consumer` and `Producer`) is a strictly
   dominated API rung — `reserve_slot_with` does everything it does,
   never worse and sometimes better — and its naive "loop on the
   fallible call" usage carries a measurable latency + jitter cost.
   Consider removing both.
   - **What we measured.** iiac-perf's zcr round-trip benches
     (`-d 60`, unpinned 3900X, one message in flight) compare the
     tiers under real cross-core traffic. `zcr-raw-2t` (main loops on
     `reserve_slot` while waiting for the reply) lands a body mean
     ~130 ns, multimodal across ~120–150 ns, trimmed `min..n2` stdev
     ~15 ns. `zcr-with-2t` / `zcr-spin-2t` (same wait via
     `reserve_slot_with` / `reserve_slot_spin`) land ~110 ns, body
     clustered at 100/109/110 ns, trimmed stdev ~7 ns. So the raw
     retry pattern costs ~+30 ns and ~2× the body jitter. `with` and
     `spin` are statistically identical, as expected —
     `reserve_slot_spin` *is* `reserve_slot_with(policy::spin)`.
   - **Why (we think).** The only code difference on the wait path is
     that `reserve_slot`-in-a-loop re-reads the caller-*owned* index
     (`consumer_idx` on the read side, `producer_idx` on the write
     side) on every iteration, because each call reloads it;
     `reserve_slot_with` loads the owned index once and re-reads only
     the peer's index per attempt (its doc already states this).
     A relaxed atomic load can't be hoisted/coalesced out of a loop
     by LLVM, so the naive retry genuinely issues the extra load each
     spin. We think that lengthens and de-tightens the spin, so it
     samples the peer's store later and less regularly — a higher
     mean and a multimodal body in ~10 ns steps (≈ one Zen 2
     cross-core coherence hop). The two indices are each
     `CacheAligned` on separate lines, so this is **not** false
     sharing between them.
   - **Current thinking.** `reserve_slot` ≡
     `reserve_slot_with(|_| false)` (give up after the first failed
     attempt) — identical loads, identical result, and the `|_| false`
     loop folds away (worth a disasm confirm). For any retry beyond
     one probe, `_with` hoists the owned load and wins. The desirable
     always-non-blocking mode (e.g. driving the ring from a Tokio
     task) is `reserve_slot_with(|_| false)`; the better hybrid
     ("spin a bounded N, then return pending and let the runtime
     park") is `reserve_slot_with(|attempt| attempt < N)` — both
     things `reserve_slot` can't express. So the proposed shape is a
     two-rung ladder per endpoint: `reserve_slot_with` (fallible
     primitive) + `reserve_slot_spin` (forever-spin convenience),
     dropping `reserve_slot`. If a real consumer later makes
     `_with(|_| false)` common, add a thin `try_reserve_slot` alias
     (named like `Sender::try_send`) then — not before (no consumers
     exist yet).
   - **Downstream.** iiac-perf's `zcr-raw-1t` / `zcr-raw-2t` call
     `reserve_slot` directly and only exist to measure this naive
     pattern; removing the method drops (or relabels) those benches.
     Tracked on the iiac-perf side.
input.new_string: _No known bugs._

input.new_string:
scheduler timeslice, so that number measures the
  scheduler, not the ring.

## refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder

Commits:

`reserve_slot` on both endpoints was a strictly dominated API
rung: `reserve_slot_with(|_| false)` does everything it did
with identical loads, and its naive loop-on-the-fallible-call
usage carried a measured +30 ns / ~2× body jitter under
cross-core traffic (bug #1, whose raw-pattern benches live in
iiac-perf's `zcr-raw-*`). Removing it leaves a two-rung ladder
per endpoint — `reserve_slot_with` (fallible primitive) +
`reserve_slot_spin` (forever-spin convenience).

- `reserve_slot_with` is now the self-contained primitive: its
  doc absorbed the single-probe (`|_| false`) note and the
  raw-pointer / `>=`-corruption rationale the deleted method
  used to host.
- Call sites split by intent: single-probe (the `_1t` demos,
  the assertion tests, the README examples) →
  `reserve_slot_with(|_| false)`; loop-on-fallible spin waits
  (the `_2t` demos, the registry tests) → `reserve_slot_spin`.
- `threaded_spsc` keeps looping on `reserve_slot_with(|_| false)`
  so the fallible primitive still has a threaded-contention
  test distinct from `threaded_spsc_spin_variants` (which
  covers the spin rung).
- No `try_reserve_slot` alias yet: nothing outside tests makes
  `_with(|_| false)` common, so per bug #1 the alias waits for
  a real consumer.

### ring-buffer-design.md: blocking intro reconciled

The `## Blocking and user words` intro still read "the crate
never blocks and never spins" — accurate before the
wait-policy hook, but `reserve_slot_spin` spins now, so a bare
method rename would have left an inline contradiction. The
intro was reframed instead: the ring's fallible core never
blocks, and *how* to wait is injected policy (a shipped
`policy::spin` drives `reserve_slot_spin`); anything that
actually sleeps still belongs to the layer above via the
`user()` words. We think the rest of that section (the
`user()` wakeup-protocol contract) is unaffected by the API
change.

### As-built ladder

- `0.8.0` refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder

# References

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Measure the wait-policy seam in iiac-perf: raw loop vs
   `_with(closure)` vs `_spin` must be zero-cost within
   noise before the endpoints build on it (the measurement
   clause of the shipped wait-policy hook) [[11]].
2. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) owning ring endpoint +
   registry access, so the demo's ~20-line send path
   becomes ~3 lines and `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once
   inside the crate (recv safe by construction); guard
   handed back on Full [[11]].
3. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
4. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
5. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Measure the wait-policy seam in iiac-perf: raw loop vs
   `_with(closure)` vs `_spin` must be zero-cost within
   noise before the endpoints build on it (the measurement
   clause of the shipped wait-policy hook) [[11]].
2. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) owning ring endpoint +
   registry access, so the demo's ~20-line send path
   becomes ~3 lines and `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once
   inside the crate (recv safe by construction); guard
   handed back on Full [[11]].
3. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
4. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
5. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

aiTitle: Remove reserve_slot from consumer and producer

content.text:
Diff is coherent — net −74 lines, `## Bugs` now reads `_No known bugs._`. Here's the commit description for your approval.

**Title:**
```
refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder
```

**Body:**
```
`reserve_slot` was strictly dominated by
`reserve_slot_with(|_| false)` (identical loads, folds to the
same code), and its loop-on-fallible usage cost a measured
+30 ns / ~2x body jitter under cross-core traffic (bug #1).
Drop it for the two-rung ladder: `reserve_slot_with` (fallible
primitive) + `reserve_slot_spin` (forever-spin convenience).

- Cargo.toml: 0.7.3 -> 0.8.0
- src/consumer.rs: delete reserve_slot; reserve_slot_with is
  now the self-contained primitive (absorbs the |_| false
  single-probe note + raw-pointer rationale); repoint Empty,
  struct, reserve_slot_spin, and ReadSlot doc links
- src/producer.rs: same for the write side (Full, WriteSlot,
  the >=-corruption rationale)
- src/lib.rs: check_type doc; test single-probes ->
  _with(|_| false); threaded_spsc loops on _with(|_| false)
- src/registry.rs: test spin-loops -> reserve_slot_spin; drop
  now-unused Empty/Full imports
- src/pool.rs: alloc doc cross-reference
- src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs: _1t -> _with(|_| false), _2t ->
  reserve_slot_spin; module doc
- examples/readme.rs: _with(|_| false)
- README.md: API line + four snippets
- notes/bugs.md: remove fixed bug #1
- notes/todo.md: Done entry [[16]]; two incidental mentions
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: chores section + blocking-intro
  reconciliation subsection
- notes/ring-buffer-design.md: API section -> two-rung ladder;
  reconcile the "never spins" blocking intro

BREAKING-CHANGE: Consumer/Producer::reserve_slot removed; use
reserve_slot_with(|_| false) or reserve_slot_spin.
```

This is a single-commit close-out, so I'll push **Keep separate** to `main` (one `0.8.0` commit). `vc-x1 push` will re-run preflight (fmt/clippy/test), inject the `ochid:` trailer, and finalize `.claude`.

Approve this description (and the push to `main`), or want changes?

aiTitle: Remove reserve_slot from consumer and producer

content.text:
Both backfills done. That adds two more touches to `chores-01.md`, so let me update the commit body's chores bullet accordingly. Here's the **final description**:

**Title:**
```
refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder
```

**Body:**
```
`reserve_slot` was strictly dominated by
`reserve_slot_with(|_| false)` (identical loads, folds to the
same code), and its loop-on-fallible usage cost a measured
+30 ns / ~2x body jitter under cross-core traffic (bug #1).
Drop it for the two-rung ladder: `reserve_slot_with` (fallible
primitive) + `reserve_slot_spin` (forever-spin convenience).

- Cargo.toml: 0.7.3 -> 0.8.0
- src/consumer.rs: delete reserve_slot; reserve_slot_with is
  now the self-contained primitive (absorbs the |_| false
  single-probe note + raw-pointer rationale); repoint Empty,
  struct, reserve_slot_spin, and ReadSlot doc links
- src/producer.rs: same for the write side (Full, WriteSlot,
  the >=-corruption rationale)
- src/lib.rs: check_type doc; test single-probes ->
  _with(|_| false); threaded_spsc loops on _with(|_| false)
- src/registry.rs: test spin-loops -> reserve_slot_spin; drop
  now-unused Empty/Full imports
- src/pool.rs: alloc doc cross-reference
- src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs: _1t -> _with(|_| false), _2t ->
  reserve_slot_spin; module doc
- examples/readme.rs: _with(|_| false)
- README.md: API line + four snippets
- notes/bugs.md: remove fixed bug #1
- notes/todo.md: Done entry [[16]]; two incidental mentions
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: new chores section + blocking-intro
  reconciliation subsection; backfill seam-lines Commits: [[31]];
  add the missing section for 35f144b
- notes/ring-buffer-design.md: API section -> two-rung ladder;
  reconcile the "never spins" blocking intro

BREAKING-CHANGE: Consumer/Producer::reserve_slot removed; use
reserve_slot_with(|_| false) or reserve_slot_spin.
```

Push shape: single-commit **Keep separate** to `main`. Approve and I'll run `vc-x1 push main`.

input.command: vc-x1 push --help 2>&1 | head -80

content.content:
vc-x1 0.67.0

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-app:      Commit the app repo
          - commit-claude:   Commit the `.claude` session repo (skipped if
            empty)
          - bookmark-both:   Advance both bookmarks to `@-`
          - push-app:        `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - finalize-claude: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <b>
            ...`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

message.content: I added a todo to remove reserve_slot_spin, let's do this as single commit v0.8.1

attachment.snippet:
1	# Todo
2	
3	This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
4	contains near term tasks with a short description and
5	uses links or reference links for more details.
6	
7	## In Progress
8	
9	_No cycle currently in progress._
10	
11	## Todo
12	
13	 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
14	 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
15	 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
16	 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
17	 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
18	 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
19	 number. Long-tail entries
20	 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
21	 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
22	 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
23	 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).
24	
25	1. Remove reserve_slot_spin: It's trivial and isn't actually
26	   useful long term.
27	2. Measure the wait-policy seam in iiac-perf: raw loop vs
28	   `_with(closure)` vs `_spin` must be zero-cost within
29	   noise before the endpoints build on it (the measurement
30	   clause of the shipped wait-policy hook) [[11]].
31	3. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
32	   send) / DescReceiver (recv) owning ring endpoint +
33	   registry access, so the demo's ~20-line send path
34	   becomes ~3 lines and `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once
35	   inside the crate (recv safe by construction); guard
36	   handed back on Full [[11]].
37	4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
38	   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
39	   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
40	   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
41	   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
42	   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
43	   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
44	   demo should show it.
45	5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
46	   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
47	   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
48	   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
49	   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
50	6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
51	   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
52	   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).
53	
54	## Ideas
55	
56	- Perf benches live in
57	  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
58	  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
59	  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
60	  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
61	  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
62	  proves necessary.
63	
64	- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
65	  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
66	  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
67	  this project in
68	  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
69	- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
70	  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
71	  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
72	  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
73	  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
74	  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
75	  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
76	  iiac-perf first.
77	- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
78	  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
79	  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
80	  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
81	  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
82	  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
83	  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
84	  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
85	  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
86	  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
87	- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
88	  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
89	  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
90	  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
91	  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
92	  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
93	- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
94	  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
95	  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
96	  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
97	  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
98	  upgrade than to walk back.
99	- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
100	  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
101	  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
102	  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
103	  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
104	- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
105	  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
106	  contracts in
107	  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
108	  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
109	  share one protocol.
110	- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
111	  protocol.
112	- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
113	  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
114	- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
115	  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
116	- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
117	  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
118	  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
119	  values from iiac-perf measurements.
120	- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
121	  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
122	  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
123	  targets would need index-width genericization.
124	- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
125	  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
126	  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
127	- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
128	  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
129	  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
130	  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
131	  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
132	  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).
133	
134	## Done
135	
136	Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
137	and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.
138	
139	- docs: messaging layer design (pools + queues) [[6]]
140	- feat: message pool allocator [[7]]
141	- feat: ring + pool demo binary [[8]]
142	- feat: demo alloc/free perf loops [[9]]
143	- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]
144	- feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring [[12]]
145	- feat: wait-policy hook + spin models [[13]]
146	- refactor: demo _closure forms + on_full params [[14]]
147	- feat: demo seam lines on diff cores, SMT last [[15]]
148	- refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder [[16]]
149	
150	# References
151	
152	[6]: chores/chores-01.md#docs-messaging-layer-design-pools--queues
153	[7]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-message-pool-allocator
154	[8]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-ring--pool-demo-binary
155	[9]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-demo-allocfree-perf-loops
156	[10]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-demo-cpu-pinned-placement-variants
157	[11]: chores/chores-01.md#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies
158	[12]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-descriptor-queues-over-the-spsc-ring
159	[13]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-wait-policy-hook--spin-models
160	[14]: chores/chores-01.md#refactor-demo-_closure-forms--on_full-params
161	[15]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-demo-seam-lines-on-diff-cores-smt-last

content.text: Not as trivial as the todo suggests — `reserve_slot_spin` has 12 call sites plus doc/README surface, and I found a **parallel `alloc_spin` on `Pool`** (same `alloc_with(policy::spin)` pattern) that raises a symmetry question. Let me read the policy module and the demo's `_spin` seam variants before I propose a plan.

content.content:
1	//! Shipped wait policies — the models users copy.
2	//!
3	//! A wait policy is any `FnMut(u32) -> bool`: the `_with`
4	//! methods ([`Pool::alloc_with`](crate::Pool::alloc_with),
5	//! [`Producer::reserve_slot_with`](crate::Producer::reserve_slot_with),
6	//! [`Consumer::reserve_slot_with`](crate::Consumer::reserve_slot_with))
7	//! call it after each failed attempt with the attempt count
8	//! (0-based, saturating); returning `false` gives up and the
9	//! operation returns its usual error.
10	//!
11	//! - The crate ships only the policies its own demo and
12	//!   tests use; compose your own (bounded retries, deadline,
13	//!   spin-then-yield) as capturing closures at the call
14	//!   site, or bake one into your own wrapper method.
15	//! - Policies are polling strategies. True blocking (futex,
16	//!   eventfd, async wakers) needs a peer wake over the
17	//!   header's user line and is a layer above this crate.
18	
19	/// Spin forever: hint the CPU and keep waiting.
20	///
21	/// `#[inline]`: non-generic, so without the hint a
22	/// monomorphized `_with` in another crate calls it out of
23	/// line — measurable on the demo's ~3 ns loops.
24	#[inline]
25	pub fn spin(_attempt: u32) -> bool {
26	    core::hint::spin_loop();
27	    true
28	}

content.text:
Before editing, here's the scope — `reserve_slot_spin` is small to define but has real reach:

**Migration** (every `reserve_slot_spin::<T>()` → `reserve_slot_with::<T>(policy::spin).unwrap()`, with `// OK: policy::spin never gives up` on non-test sites):
- `src/consumer.rs`, `src/producer.rs` — delete the method + its doc; repoint the `reserve_slot_with` doc's "convenience" reference
- `src/registry.rs` (2 test sites), `src/lib.rs` (`threaded_spsc_spin_variants`, 2 sites)
- `src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs` — **8 sites** incl. the dedicated `_spin` seam-check variants (they stay meaningful: shipped `policy::spin` fn vs inline closure), plus module doc
- `notes/ring-buffer-design.md` — the API bullets + the blocking intro I just wrote reference `reserve_slot_spin`
- `README.md` — prose line 29

`policy::spin` **stays** (public — the migration target, and `Pool::alloc_spin` uses it).

Three things to decide first:

questions.question: Pool has a parallel `alloc_spin` = `alloc_with(policy::spin)` — the exact same convenience pattern for the allocator. Remove it too in this commit?

options.label: Remove alloc_spin too
options.description: Keep the API symmetric: if the spin convenience isn't worth it on the ring, it isn't on the pool either. Migrate alloc_spin callers to alloc_with(policy::spin).unwrap(). Widens this commit but keeps Ring/Pool consistent.

options.label: Only reserve_slot_spin
options.description: Do exactly what the todo says. Leaves Pool::alloc_spin in place, creating an asymmetry (pool keeps its _spin convenience, ring endpoints don't). Narrower commit.

questions.question: Version bump? Last cycle we bumped MINOR (0.7.3->0.8.0) with a BREAKING-CHANGE trailer for removing a public method (reserve_slot). Removing reserve_slot_spin is the same kind of breaking removal.

options.label: 0.8.1 (patch, as you said)
options.description: Follow your stated version. Simple, but inconsistent with the precedent we just set (0.8.0 minor for removing reserve_slot) and understates a breaking public-API removal.

options.label: 0.9.0 (minor, breaking)
options.description: Match last cycle's convention: public method removal = minor bump + BREAKING-CHANGE trailer. Consistent semver story across the two removals.

options.label: Relabel/remove spin benches

content.content:
1	//! Demo binary: both primitives working across threads,
2	//! with throughput printed — run `cargo run --release`, or
3	//! `cargo install --path . --locked` and run
4	//! `zc-ring-x1-demo`. `-V`/`--version` prints the
5	//! version-of-record so you know exactly which build you
6	//! are testing.
7	//!
8	//! - Part 1, the ring: an SPSC pair moves typed messages
9	//!   in place (reserve → write → commit; reserve → read →
10	//!   release) — first both ends on one thread
11	//!   (the ring's own cost), then one producer thread to
12	//!   one consumer thread: unpinned, pinned to two different
13	//!   physical cores, and pinned to one physical core's two
14	//!   SMT siblings (shared L1/L2, when the CPU has SMT).
15	//! - Part 2, the pool: an allocator thread allocs and
16	//!   fills `BufSlot`s and hands them to a freer thread —
17	//!   "send" today is moving the guard (see the README's
18	//!   usage model); getting a buffer implies nothing about
19	//!   when it is sent or freed.
20	//! - The composed form — descriptors through the ring,
21	//!   payloads at rest in pool buffers — runs between them:
22	//!   alloc → into_desc → ring → resolve → free, with the
23	//!   same placement ladder as the raw ring.
24	//! - The `_closure` / `_spin` lines are the wait-policy
25	//!   seam exemplar: one workload written in all three forms
26	//!   side by side — raw (the hand-rolled wait loop, the
27	//!   fastest form we know for the shape and the baseline
28	//!   the others are measured against), `_closure` (a
29	//!   spinning closure through the `reserve_slot_with`
30	//!   hook), and `_spin` (the same hook under the shipped
31	//!   `policy::spin`). Calibrated
32	//!   measurement lives in iiac-perf; these lines are the
33	//!   eyeball check.

attachment.snippet:
1	# zc-ring-x1
2	
3	Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1
4	
5	This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
6	a bot to help develop the code.
7	
8	The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
9	
10	## Overview
11	
12	A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
13	over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
14	zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
15	`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
16	a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
17	[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
18	layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
19	processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
20	scheme, API, validation — lives in
21	[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
22	in sync with `src/`.
23	
24	- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
25	  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
26	- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
27	  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
28	- Guard API: `reserve_slot_with::<T>(policy)` on either
29	  endpoint — write → `commit()` on the producer side, read →
30	  `release()` on the consumer side; dropping a guard abandons
31	  cleanly.
32	- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
33	  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
34	  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
35	  never cause UB.
36	- An app-owned scratch line in the header (`user()` on both
37	  endpoints, 16 `AtomicU32`s): the crate zeroes it at init and
38	  never touches it again — a shared-memory home for app wakeup
39	  protocols; the crate itself never blocks or spins.
40	- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
41	  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
42	  on the non-threaded suite.
43	
44	```rust
45	use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
46	use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};
47	
48	#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
49	#[repr(C)]
50	struct Msg {
51	    seq: u64,
52	    val: u64,
53	}
54	
55	// Cache-line-aligned region: 256 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
56	#[repr(C, align(64))]
57	struct Region([u8; 512]);
58	let mut region = Region([0; 512]);
59	
60	let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
61	    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();
62	
63	let mut slot = producer.reserve_slot_with::<Msg>(|_| false).unwrap();
64	slot.seq = 1;
65	slot.val = 42;
66	slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer
67	
68	let msg = consumer.reserve_slot_with::<Msg>(|_| false).unwrap();
69	assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
70	msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
71	```
72	
73	Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
74	shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
75	hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
76	[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).
77	
78	## Message pool
79	
80	The companion allocation primitive: fixed-size
81	cache-line-aligned buffers over a caller-provided region,
82	decoupling "get a message" from "send it" — allocate a
83	buffer, hold it as long as you like, free (or eventually
84	send) it whenever. Design and roadmap:
85	[Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues](notes/ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
86	
87	- Intrusive LIFO free-stack with zero per-buffer overhead: a
88	  *free* buffer's first word is its next-link; an allocated
89	  buffer carries no header at all.
90	- `alloc::<T>()` returns a `BufSlot<T>` — the ownership
91	  token for one buffer, `Deref`/`DerefMut` to `T` in place.
92	  The pop is single-popper Treiber (`&mut self`, no ABA):
93	  one allocator per pool.
94	- `BufSlot::free` is an MPSC push: whichever thread or
95	  process ends up holding the guard may free it.
96	- `Pool::init` creates and validates a fresh region;
97	  `Pool::attach` joins one initialized elsewhere — `unsafe`
98	  like `Ring::attach` (the caller vouches for the mapping),
99	  and its contract carries the single-allocator rule: at
100	  most one attached handle allocates, any handle frees.
101	- Messaging aside, a pool is a fast fixed-size object
102	  allocator in its own right: O(1) alloc/free, no locks, no
103	  syscalls, cache-aligned `T`s with LIFO reuse keeping the
104	  working set hot — usable today for app-owned objects that
105	  never travel.
106	- `BufSlot` does not borrow the pool — any number of
107	  allocated buffers live at once; dropping without `free`
108	  leaks the buffer (documented, like the ring's abandon but
109	  with the opposite consequence).
110	- Free-stack words are untrusted shared memory: pops are
111	  bounds-checked and corruption degrades to `Exhausted`,
112	  never out-of-bounds access.
113	- Same init/attach handshake, all-atomic header, and
114	  hostile-peer posture as the ring.
115	
116	```rust
117	use zc_ring_x1::Pool;
118	use zerocopy::{FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};
119	
120	#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout)]
121	#[repr(C)]
122	struct Msg {
123	    seq: u64,
124	    val: u64,
125	}
126	
127	// Cache-line-aligned region: 128 B header + 4 bufs × 64 B.
128	#[repr(C, align(64))]
129	struct Region([u8; 384]);
130	let mut region = Region([0; 384]);
131	
132	let mut pool = Pool::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap();
133	
134	let mut a = pool.alloc::<Msg>().unwrap();
135	let mut b = pool.alloc::<Msg>().unwrap(); // many live at once
136	a.seq = 1;
137	b.seq = 2;
138	b.free(); // any order, from any thread the guard moved to
139	a.free();
140	```
141	
142	## Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle
143	
144	Who may do what, per object and per buffer state. The tests
145	exercise exactly what this model permits; anything outside it
146	is a contract violation even where the compiler cannot reject
147	it.
148	
149	**Roles.** Roles are per *object*, and one thread may hold
150	several roles across objects (a typical sender is one pool's
151	allocator and one ring's producer):
152	
153	- **Ring** — exactly one producer and one consumer, ever
154	  (SPSC): in-process, `split()` hands out each endpoint
155	  once; cross-process, one producing and one consuming
156	  process by contract. An endpoint reserves at most one
157	  slot at a time (guard holds the endpoint borrow).
158	- **Pool** — exactly one *allocator* at a time (the
159	  single-popper contract on `Pool::attach`; in-process,
160	  `alloc` takes `&mut self`), and **any number of freers**:
161	  whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it, from any thread or
162	  process attached to the pool.
163	
164	**Buffer lifecycle.** A pool buffer is always in exactly one
165	state, with one party allowed to touch it:
166	
167	- **free** — on the free-stack; the pool protocol owns it.
168	  Nobody reads or writes it except through alloc/free
169	  machinery (its first word is the next-link).
170	- **allocated** — popped by `alloc`, owned by the `BufSlot`
171	  holder: exclusive read/write through the guard, held as
172	  long as desired, moved between threads freely (`Send`).
173	  No other party — including the pool — may touch the
174	  bytes.
175	- **in-flight** — the descriptor form: `into_desc` consumes
176	  the guard and ownership travels in the returned `Desc`
177	  (typically through a ring). The sender must no longer
178	  touch the buffer; whoever `resolve`s the descriptor owns
179	  it, exactly once.
180	- **freed** — `BufSlot::free` pushes it back; ownership
181	  returns to the pool protocol the instant the CAS lands.
182	  Use-after-free of the guard is unrepresentable (`free`
183	  consumes it); dropping without `free` leaks the buffer.
184	
185	**What "send" means.** Two forms compose:
186	
187	- Moving the `BufSlot` itself (an ordinary Rust move;
188	  `Send` lets it cross threads or a std channel).
189	- The descriptor flow (0.7.0): register the pool in a
190	  per-process `PoolRegistry`, convert the guard to an
191	  8-byte `Desc` with `into_desc`, and send *that* through
192	  a ring as an ordinary POD message; the receiver

226	Producer — reserve_slot_with/commit, in place:
227	
228	- `producer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_full) ->
229	  Result<WriteSlot<'_, T>, Full>` — next free slot as `&mut T`
230	  (zerocopy `FromBytes + IntoBytes + KnownLayout`), retrying
231	  under the injected wait policy — `on_full(attempt) -> bool`,
232	  `|_| false` for a single non-blocking probe. `T` geometry is
233	  asserted against the slot size on each call (a mismatch is a
234	  programming error, so it panics; typed endpoints that check
235	  once are a follow-on — see [todo.md](todo.md)).
236	- `producer.reserve_slot_spin::<T>() -> WriteSlot<'_, T>` — the
237	  forever-spin convenience (`reserve_slot_with` under
238	  `policy::spin`), so no `Result`.
239	- `WriteSlot::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
240	  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
241	  abandons the reservation (nothing published).
242	
243	Consumer — reserve_slot_with/release, in place:
244	
245	- `consumer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_empty) ->
246	  Result<ReadSlot<'_, T>, Empty>` — oldest unread slot as
247	  `&T`, retrying under the injected wait policy (`|_| false`
248	  for a single non-blocking probe).
249	- `consumer.reserve_slot_spin::<T>() -> ReadSlot<'_, T>` — the
250	  forever-spin convenience, so no `Result`.
251	- `ReadSlot::release(self)` — frees the slot
252	  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
253	  leaves the slot unread — the next reservation returns it
254	  again.
255	
256	Each side reserves at most **one slot at a time**: the guard
257	holds the endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second reservation
258	while a guard is live is a compile error, not a runtime state.
259	
260	Both endpoints also expose `user() -> &[AtomicU32; 16]` — the
261	header's app-owned scratch line; see
262	[Blocking and user words](#blocking-and-user-words).
263	
264	Guard internals: both guards hold **raw slot pointers** and
265	mint `&T` / `&mut T` per access via Deref. A reference *field*
266	would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
267	`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
268	the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
269	protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
270	(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).
271	
272	Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
273	the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
274	single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.
275	
276	## Blocking and user words
277	
278	The ring's fallible core never blocks: `reserve_slot_with`
279	returning [`Full`]/[`Empty`] under a give-up policy (`|_| false`)
280	is the primitive. *How* to wait on it — spin, yield, park,
281	await — is injected policy: a shipped [`policy::spin`] drives
282	the [`reserve_slot_spin`] convenience, but anything that
283	actually sleeps belongs to the layer above (an embedded caller
284	may WFE on an interrupt, an async runtime wants a waker, a
285	pinned busy-poll thread wants a pure spin; no single

file.content:
Producer — reserve_slot_with/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_full) ->
  Result<WriteSlot<'_, T>, Full>` — next free slot as `&mut T`
  (zerocopy `FromBytes + IntoBytes + KnownLayout`), retrying
  under the injected wait policy — `on_full(attempt) -> bool`,
  `|_| false` for a single non-blocking probe. `T` geometry is
  asserted against the slot size on each call (a mismatch is a
  programming error, so it panics; typed endpoints that check
  once are a follow-on — see [todo.md](todo.md)).
- `producer.reserve_slot_spin::<T>() -> WriteSlot<'_, T>` — the
  forever-spin convenience (`reserve_slot_with` under
  `policy::spin`), so no `Result`.
- `WriteSlot::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — reserve_slot_with/release, in place:

- `consumer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_empty) ->
  Result<ReadSlot<'_, T>, Empty>` — oldest unread slot as
  `&T`, retrying under the injected wait policy (`|_| false`
  for a single non-blocking probe).
- `consumer.reserve_slot_spin::<T>() -> ReadSlot<'_, T>` — the
  forever-spin convenience, so no `Result`.
- `ReadSlot::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next reservation returns it
  again.

Each side reserves at most **one slot at a time**: the guard
holds the endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second reservation
while a guard is live is a compile error, not a runtime state.

Both endpoints also expose `user() -> &[AtomicU32; 16]` — the
header's app-owned scratch line; see
[Blocking and user words](#blocking-and-user-words).

Guard internals: both guards hold **raw slot pointers** and
mint `&T` / `&mut T` per access via Deref. A reference *field*
would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Remove reserve_slot_spin: It's trivial and isn't actually
   useful long term.
2. Measure the wait-policy seam in iiac-perf: raw loop vs
   `_with(closure)` vs `_spin` must be zero-cost within
   noise before the endpoints build on it (the measurement
   clause of the shipped wait-policy hook) [[11]].
3. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) owning ring endpoint +
   registry access, so the demo's ~20-line send path
   becomes ~3 lines and `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once
   inside the crate (recv safe by construction); guard
   handed back on Full [[11]].
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- docs: messaging layer design (pools + queues) [[6]]
- feat: message pool allocator [[7]]
- feat: ring + pool demo binary [[8]]
- feat: demo alloc/free perf loops [[9]]
- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]
- feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring [[12]]
- feat: wait-policy hook + spin models [[13]]
- refactor: demo _closure forms + on_full params [[14]]
- feat: demo seam lines on diff cores, SMT last [[15]]
- refactor: drop reserve_slot, keep _with ladder [[16]]

[7]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-message-pool-allocator

[11]: chores/chores-01.md#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies
[12]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-descriptor-queues-over-the-spsc-ring
[13]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-wait-policy-hook--spin-models
[14]: chores/chores-01.md#refactor-demo-_closure-forms--on_full-params
[15]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-demo-seam-lines-on-diff-cores-smt-last

- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.
